specification.topic-statement: The wait synchronization system blocks the calling agent until a message arrives from any sender or until a specified set of target agents each reach a terminal state.

scope.in-scope: Two wait modes (next-message and fan-in), PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY setup and teardown, poll loop with select and timeout, interrupted-turn detection, fan-in state tracking per target agent, and result rendering.
scope.boundary: Whether a message exists in the mailbox, and reading or deleting that message, is delegated to the mail storage layer. This topic queries the mailbox but does not implement it.
Whether a target agent is idle or dead is read from the agent record. How that record is updated is outside this scope.

How the REPL detects that a background tool thread has completed and invokes the completion callback is handled by the REPL event loop. This topic participates in that protocol but does not implement it.
How the formatted result strings are displayed to the user is handled by the event rendering layer.

field.name: from identifier
field.note: UUID of the agent that sent the message; null on timeout or error

field.name: message
field.note: message body, or a status string such as 'Timeout' or 'Interrupted'

field.name: agent name
field.note: display name of the target agent; 'undefined' if lookup failed

field.name: status
field.note: one of: running, received, idle, dead

field.name: message
field.note: message body; only present when status is 'received'

field.name: entries
field.note: one entry per target agent, in the order targets were specified

behavior.invocation: The wait command is invoked with an optional timeout (in seconds) and an optional list of target agent identifiers. When no targets are listed, next-message mode is used. When one or more targets are listed, fan-in mode is used. Execution is dispatched to a background thread so the REPL remains responsive. The calling agent's state transitions to executing-tool for the duration of the wait.
behavior.next-message-mode:
In next-message mode, the system waits for any message sent to the calling agent's mailbox from any sender.
A PostgreSQL notification channel is registered for the calling agent. The mailbox is checked immediately before entering the poll loop. If a message is already present, it is consumed and the wait completes without entering the loop.
The poll loop repeats until a message is received, the timeout expires, or the interrupted flag is set.
Each iteration: 1. The interrupted flag is checked. If true, the wait ends with the text "Interrupted" as the result message and a null sender. 2. Elapsed time is calculated using a monotonic clock. If the timeout has expired, the wait ends with the text "Timeout" as the result message and a null sender. 3. A file descriptor watch is set on the database connection socket with a timeout capped at 50 milliseconds. If the watch returns a socket error other than an interrupted system call, the loop exits with the result message set to "Select failed" and the sender set to null. An interrupted system call causes the iteration to restart. 4. Pending database notifications are consumed from the connection. 5. The mailbox is checked. If a message is present, it is consumed, the sender identifier and message body are recorded, and the loop exits. The consumed message is then deleted from the mailbox; if the deletion fails, the failure is silently ignored and the message remains in the inbox.
The mailbox is checked after every socket event and after every 50 millisecond poll interval expiration, whether or not a notification was received. The notification is used only to wake the socket watch early; the actual message is always retrieved by querying the mailbox.
The content of any PostgreSQL notification payload is stored in a local context structure but never read or acted upon. Only the fact that the socket became readable triggers the mailbox check.
The notification channel registration is cancelled before the function returns, regardless of the exit reason.
behavior.fan-in-mode:
In fan-in mode, the system waits until each target agent has either sent a message to the calling agent's mailbox, entered an idle state, or died.
A notification channel is registered for the calling agent. A notification channel is also registered for each target agent. The result entries array is initialized with one entry per target agent, all in status "running" and with name "undefined". A batch lookup of target agent display names is attempted; if successful, the "undefined" placeholders are replaced. If the name lookup fails for any reason, the "undefined" values are retained without error. When the batch lookup succeeds but an individual entry's name is null, the "undefined" placeholder for that target is also retained.
The notification channel registration for each target agent is performed without checking the result. If a LISTEN fails for one or more targets, the system continues with those channels unregistered. Notifications from those targets will never wake the socket watch; the wait will rely on the 50-millisecond polling interval to detect their state changes. All channels, including those that were never successfully registered, are submitted to UNLISTEN on teardown.
The poll loop repeats until all targets are resolved, the timeout expires, or the interrupted flag is set.
Each iteration: 1. The interrupted flag is checked. If true, the loop exits immediately. Unresolved targets remain in "running" status. 2. Elapsed time is calculated. If the timeout has expired, the loop exits. Unresolved targets remain in "running" status. 3. Each target's entry is updated: - If the entry's status is already not "running", it is skipped (the check is idempotent once resolved). - The calling agent's mailbox is checked for a message from this specific target. If found, the message is consumed, the entry status is set to "received", and the message body is stored. The consumed message is then deleted; if the deletion fails, the failure is silently ignored. - If no message was found, the target agent's record is retrieved. If the agent's status is "dead", the entry status is set to "dead". If the agent is idle, the entry status is set to "idle". 4. If all entries have a status other than "running", the loop exits. 5. A file descriptor watch is set on the database connection socket with a timeout capped at 50 milliseconds. If the watch returns a socket error other than an interrupted system call, the loop exits with entries in their current state and no result message set. An interrupted system call causes the iteration to restart. 6. Pending database notifications are consumed.
Because the socket watch is capped at 50 milliseconds, a timeout value shorter than 50 milliseconds may not be honored precisely. The first iteration will block for up to 50 milliseconds before recalculating elapsed time.
The notification channel registrations for the calling agent and all target agents are cancelled before the function returns.
behavior.result-rendering:
After the background thread completes, the completion callback renders the result to the calling agent's scrollback.
For next-message mode: the sender identifier and message body are rendered as a single entry.
For fan-in mode: a "Fan-in results:" header is rendered, followed by one line per target entry showing the agent identifier, status, and — when the status is "received" — the message body.
behavior.database-connection-failure:
If the database socket file descriptor is unavailable after notification channel registration, the notification registration is cancelled and the wait completes immediately. For next-message mode, the result message is set to "Database connection error" with a null sender. For fan-in mode, all notification channels are cancelled and the function returns with entries in their initialized state.
Database connection failure causes the wait to complete with a sentinel message rather than returning an error result to the REPL. The user sees a result entry rather than an error notification.

state-transitions.transition: Calling agent returns to idle state (via REPL completion callback)
Target entry status transitions from "running" to "received"
Target entry status transitions from "running" to "idle"
Target entry status transitions from "running" to "dead"
